Job continues speaking about the prosperity of the wicked. He had shown that many live comfortably who are ungodly and irreverent. Here he shows that many who live in open defiance of all laws of justice succeed in wicked practices, and we do not see them held accountable in this world. He notices those who do wrong under the guise of law and authority, and robbers, those who do wrong by force. He says, “God does not consider their folly;” that is, he does not immediately send his judgments or make them examples, so as to reveal their foolishness to all the world. But someone who gains riches unjustly will be a fool at the end.

See how much care and effort wicked people take to achieve their wicked goals; let it shame our negligence and laziness in doing good. See how much effort those put into making provisions for the flesh to fulfill its desires: effort to achieve, and then to hide what will ultimately lead to death and hell. Less effort would subdue and crucify the flesh, and be life and heaven instead. Shame came with sin, and everlasting shame is at the end of it. See the misery of sinners; they are exposed to constant fear. Yet see their folly; they are afraid of being watched by people, but have no fear of God’s gaze, which is always upon them. They are not afraid of doing things that they are afraid of being known to do.

Sometimes how gradual is the decline, how quiet the departure of a wicked person! How quickly their cruelties and oppressions are forgotten! They are taken away with other people, like a harvester gathers ears of corn as he finds them. There will often appear much that resembles the mistaken view of Providence Job takes in this chapter. But we are taught by inspired words that these notions are formed from ignorance, based on incomplete understanding. God’s providence in the affairs of men is always just and wise. Let us apply this whenever the Lord may test us. He cannot do wrong. The unequaled sorrows of the Son of God when he was on earth would confuse the mind unless viewed in this way. But when we behold him as the sinner's substitute, bearing the curse, we can explain why he should endure that wrath which was due to sin, so that Divine justice might be satisfied and his people saved.
